paper_title: MedSteer: Counterfactual Endoscopic Synthesis via Training-Free Activation Steering

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Generative diffusion models are increasingly used for medical imaging data augmentation, but text prompting cannot produce causal training data. Re-prompting rerolls the entire generation trajectory, altering anatomy, texture, and background. Inversion-based editing methods introduce reconstruction error that causes structural drift. We propose MedSteer, a training-free activation-steering framework for endoscopic synthesis. MedSteer identifies a pathology vector for each contrastive prompt pair in the cross-attention layers of a diffusion transformer. At inference time, it steers image activations along this vector, generating counterfactual pairs from scratch where the only difference is the steered concept. All other structure is preserved by construction. We evaluate MedSteer across three experiments on Kvasir v3 and HyperKvasir. On counterfactual generation across three clinical concept pairs, MedSteer achieves flip rates of 0.800, 0.925, and 0.950, outperforming the best inversion-based baseline in both concept flip rate and structural preservation. On dye disentanglement, MedSteer achieves 75% dye removal against 20% (PnP) and 10% (h-Edit). On downstream polyp detection, augmenting with MedSteer counterfactual pairs achieves ViT AUC of 0.9755 versus 0.9083 for quantity-matched re-prompting, confirming that counterfactual structure drives the gain. Code is at link https://github.com/phamtrongthang123/medsteer

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 6 2 0 2 7 ] . [ 1 6 6 0 7 0 . 3 0 6 2 : r MedSteer: Counterfactual Endoscopic Synthesis via Training-Free Activation Steering Trong-Thang Pham, Loc Nguyen, Anh Nguyen, Hien Nguyen, Ngan Le No Institute Given Abstract. Generative diffusion models are increasingly used for medical imaging data augmentation, but text prompting cannot produce causal training data. Re-prompting rerolls the entire generation trajectory, altering anatomy, texture, and background. Inversion-based editing methods introduce reconstruction error that causes structural drift. We propose MedSteer, training-free activation-steering framework for endoscopic synthesis. MedSteer identifies pathology vector for each contrastive prompt pair in the cross-attention layers of diffusion transformer. At inference time, it steers image activations along this vector, generating counterfactual pairs from scratch where the only difference is the steered concept. All other structure is preserved by construction. We evaluate MedSteer across three experiments on Kvasir v3 and HyperKvasir. On counterfactual generation across three clinical concept pairs, MedSteer achieves flip rates of 0.800, 0.925, and 0.950, outperforming the best inversion-based baseline in both concept flip rate and structural preservation. On dye disentanglement, MedSteer achieves 75% dye removal against 20% (PnP) and 10% (h-Edit). On downstream polyp detection, augmenting with MedSteer counterfactual pairs achieves ViT AUC of 0.9755 versus 0.9083 for quantity-matched re-prompting, confirming that counterfactual structure drives the gain. Code will be made publicly available upon acceptance. Keywords: Endoscopy Generative AI Counterfactual Explanation Representation Steering Data Augmentation Causal AI

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: A key challenge in endoscopic image analysis is training pathology detectors that respond to disease-specific features rather than confounding anatomy. natural solution is to generate anatomy-matched pairs of diseased and healthy images so the detector learns only the targeted concept. Diffusion models can in principle generate such pairs, but text-to-image re-prompting between any two concepts (polyp to normal, colitis to healthy mucosa, or dyed to undyed tissue) rerolls the entire generation trajectory, producing completely different image. Diffusion-based editing methods [22,31] start from source image but rely on Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIM) inversion, an approximation that introduces reconstruction error. RadEdit [27] compounds this with mandatory 2 Pham et al. mask annotations per region. No workaround closes this gap. Even improved inversion [21] cannot eliminate drift, because re-entering trajectory is inherently approximate. The requirement that non-targeted structure1 be exactly preserved, not merely approximated, remains unmet. We propose MedSteer, training-free framework requiring no fine-tuning, source images, or annotations. It estimates pathology vectors from contrastive prompt pairs in the cross-attention space of frozen diffusion transformer and steers activations along those vectors at inference time. With shared noise seed, both images traverse the same trajectory, making all non-targeted concept structure identical by construction. Our contributions are: 1. We introduce cosine-similarity-derived activation steering mechanism that steers clinical concepts by removing only the concept-aligned component of each cross-attention token via per-token gate. This gate reveals exactly where and when the model steers, providing built-in spatial interpretability absent from inversion-based approaches. 2. We demonstrate inversion-free counterfactual pair generation from scratch. Both images share the same noise trajectory through frozen model. MedSteer outperforms existing state-of-the-art editing methods in non-targeted concept structure preservation.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Diffusion-Based Editing and Medical Counterfactual Generation. Trainingbased editing approaches fine-tune the denoiser or auxiliary networks [14,13,16], while training-free methods manipulate attention maps [4,24], improve inversion fidelity [21,12,3], or use masks to localise edits [1,6]. In the medical domain, counterfactual and pathology-removal methods [25,29,9,11] and RadEdit [27] all follow the same generate invert edit pipeline, inheriting the fundamental limitation that DDIM inversion is an approximation and background drift is bounded but never eliminated. MedSteer avoids this pipeline by generating both images from the same noise seed, guaranteeing non-targeted structure preservation. Concept Control via Latent Directions. Recent work shows that diffusion activations encode semantically meaningful linear directions [16,23,30,31] that can steer generation. SDID [17] and SAeUron [7] require extensive training, while concept-erasure methods fine-tune weights [10,15] or Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) adapters [20,19], all prohibitive when labelled medical data is scarce. None has been applied to medical imaging, where class labels encode entangled visual attributes that text cannot decompose, and utility must be validated on downstream clinical tasks rather than perceptual metrics alone. MedSteer addresses this gap in that it requires no training, operates on entangled medical class labels, and validates utility on downstream detection task. 1 By non-targeted we mean all structure unrelated to the steered concept: anatomy, texture, and background that should remain identical between the pair. MedSteer: Counterfactual Endoscopic Synthesis 3 Fig. 1: MedSteer method pipeline. A&N: Add & Norm. FF: Feed Forward. CA: Cross-Attention. SA: Self-Attention. (a) Offline Pathology Vector Estimation: CA features hl,t,z are collected from the frozen DiT for positive l,t and hneg and negative prompts across random seeds. Mean step yields hpos l,t . &N (Subtract & Normalize) step then produces the unit pathology vector vl,t (dyed lifted polyp polyp). (b) Inference-Time Steering: Unsteered Inference runs the frozen DiT unmodified. Steered Inference applies Spatially Selective Pathology Steering (SSPS) at layers {Ls, . . . , Le} across all denoising steps. Inside SSPS, CSG (Cosine-similarity gate) produces the per-token score σl,t, which is scaled by α and fed to an Update step that subtracts the aligned component from hl,t, yielding the counterfactual activation l,t. While both inference branches are shown together here, only the desired branch is executed in practice.

sections.title: 3 Methodology
sections.content: In this section, we describe MedSteer through three stages: the backbone and intervention point (Sec. 3.1), Pathology Vector Estimation (Sec. 3.2), and Spatially Selective Pathology Steering (SSPS) (Sec. 3.3).

sections.title: 3.1 Backbone and Intervention Point
sections.content: MedSteer builds on PixArt-α [5], Diffusion Transformer (DiT) [26] with =28 transformer layers. Each layer contributes self-attention, cross-attention (CA), and feed-forward terms to the residual stream. The CA output is the sole conduit through which textual semantics reach image tokens, making it the natural intervention point. direction estimated from contrastive text prompts can be subtracted there to suppress clinical concept without touching anatomy encoded elsewhere. We therefore intervene on CA outputs at layers {Ls, . . . , Le} across all denoising timesteps, with the layer range determined empirically in Sec. 4.5. 4 Pham et al.

sections.title: 3.2 Pathology Vector Estimation
sections.content: pos, (z) Contrastive prompt design. clinical concept is pathological class label (e.g., Polyp, Normal Cecum, Ulcerative Colitis, Normal Z-line, Esophagitis, Dyed Lifted Polyp). For each , we assemble positive/negative prompt pairs {(T (z) neg)} that differ only in the presence of (e.g., [context] of dyed lifted polyp vs. [context] of polyp), with context phrasing (e.g., An endoscopic image) and random seed varied across pairs to marginalise out texture and viewpoint variation. Pathology vector. For each prompt pair, we perform forward passes through the frozen model. Mean: the CA features hl,t,n are averaged over all seeds and all spatial tokens, yielding hpos l,t , hneg l,t Rdl per layer and timestep {1, . . . , }. Subtract & Normalize: following the mean-difference principle [32], the l,t L2-normalised contrastive difference gives the pathology vector vl,t = l,t 2 This yields unit vector per layer and timestep that captures the shared semantic difference attributable to . The per-timestep parameterization is necessary because the diffusion process encodes concepts differently across denoising steps, with early timesteps establishing global structure and later steps refining finegrained details. Since vl,t is concept-specific rather than image-specific, it is computed once offline and reused across all inference calls at no additional cost. . l,t hneg hpos l,t hneg hpos

sections.title: 3.3 Spatially Selective Pathology Steering (SSPS)
sections.content: Counterfactual setup. Unlike inversion-based methods, MedSteer requires no source image, no mask annotation, and no fine-tuning: both images are generated from scratch using the same noise seed z. Both branches share the same prompt Tpos. While Unsteered Inference runs the frozen DiT unmodified, Steered Inference inserts SSPS at layers {Ls, . . . , Le} at every denoising timestep to suppress the target pathology. Because both branches share the same seed z, any structural difference between the paired outputs arises solely from the SSPS intervention, making the comparison true minimal-edit counterfactual. Cosine-similarity gate (CSG). Because pathological changes are spatially focal, global scalar subtraction would over-suppress non-target tokens. We score each tokens alignment with the pathology concept via cos(hl,t, vl,t) = hl,t, vl,t/hl,t2, where hl,t denotes the activation of any visual token at layer l, timestep t. We deliberately drop the 1/hl,t2 normalizer, arriving at the dot product hl,t, vl,t, so that tokens expressing the concept more strongly receive proportionally stronger steering: σl,t = max(cid:0)hl,t, vl,t, 0(cid:1). The max(, 0) operator ensures that only tokens positively aligned with vl,t are modified, and tokens with zero or negative alignment pass through unchanged. We then update each tokens activation by subtracting the pathology-aligned component scaled by α (the Update step in Fig. 1), leaving all orthogonal components (anatomy, texture, viewpoint) intact: l,t = hl,t α σl,t vl,t, where α is the steering strength. MedSteer: Counterfactual Endoscopic Synthesis

sections.title: 4.1 Experimental Setup
sections.content: Datasets and model. We use Kvasir v3 [28] (8,000 gastrointestinal endoscopy images, eight classes) for generative training, pathology vector construction, and all generative evaluations. HyperKvasir [2] provides the held-out test set for downstream polyp detection. The generative backbone is PixArt-α [5] fine-tuned with LoRA (rank r=64) on Kvasir. Pathology vector construction. For each concept pair (Tpos, Tneg) we generate =50 images per prompt and apply the method in Sec. 3 across layers {8, . . . , 16} and all timesteps to obtain vl,t for each setting. Baselines and metrics. We compare Re-prompting (i.e. generate another image using different prompt with the same random seed), and two DDIMinversion-based editing methods: Plug-and-Play (PnP) [31] and h-Edit [22] (RadEdit [27] is excluded as it requires per-image mask annotations). pretrained oracle classifier (area under the ROC curve, AUC = 0.98) measures concept flip rate (fraction of outputs no longer classified as the source class) and confidence shift (mean drop in source-class probability). Background preservation is assessed via Bg-LPIPS [34], Bg-SSIM, and Bg-PSNR (each restricted to the background, i.e., non-lesion, region isolated by UNet++ [35] (Dice = 0.912)) for counterfactual experiments. Dye disentanglement additionally reports results under Segformer [33] (Dice = 0.921).

sections.title: 4.2 Downstream Polyp Detection
sections.content: In the first experiment, we assess whether MedSteer-generated augmentations improve downstream polyp detection. We train binary polyp detectors (ConvNeXt [18] and ViT [8]) under five augmentation methods: Real-only (20 images/class), Re-prompting, PnP, h-Edit, and MedSteer. Each augmention method adds =1,000 synthetic images so that differences reflect augmentation structure, not quantity. Since HyperKvasir is an extension of Kvasir, we remove all exact and near-duplicate matches from the test set before evaluation to ensure strictly out-of-distribution assessment. Table 1 shows that MedSteer achieves Table 1: Downstream polyp detection on HyperKvasir (N =1,000 synthetic images per condition). CF: counterfactual generation. ConvNeXt ViT Methods CF? F1 AUC F1 AUC Real-only Re-prompting PnP h-Edit MedSteer 0.8977.019 0.8469.023 0.9062.022 0.8942.026 0.9038.016 0.8714.020 0.9147.018 0.9083.022 0.9174.014 0.9086.017 0.9437.013 0.9518.017 0.9097.015 0.8902.019 0.9295.016 0.9312.020 0.9263.012 0.9341.015 0.9591.011 0.9755. 6 Pham et al. Fig. 2: Qualitative comparison across concept pairs. Rows: Unsteered, PnP, hEdit, and MedSteer (Ours). Columns show Polyp Normal Cecum, Ulcerative Colitis Normal Cecum, Esophagitis Normal Z-line, and dye steerings. the best AUC under both backbones. This proves that paired supervision forces detectors to learn useful pathology features that generalize well into OOD setting.

sections.title: 4.3 Counterfactual Generation Across Clinical Concepts
sections.content: In the second experiment, we evaluate concept steering quality across multiple clinical concept pairs, measuring concept flip rate and background preservation. We evaluate three concept pairs: (A) Polyp Normal Cecum, (B) Ulcerative Colitis Normal Cecum, and (C) Esophagitis Normal Z-line (N =1,000 images, α=2.5). Background metrics are reported for pair (A) only. MedSteer leads on pair (A): Flip = 0.800, p=0.721, Bg-LPIPS = 0.1449, and best Bg-SSIM and Bg-PSNR. Inversion-based methods accumulate DDIM reconstruction error, degrading both concept-change rate and background fidelity. Pairs (B) and (C) yield flip rates of 0.925 and 0.950, confirming generalisation across clinical concepts and anatomical sites (Table 2 and Fig. 2).

sections.title: 4.4 Dye Disentanglement
sections.content: In the third experiment, we test disentanglement of co-occurring attributes (polyp morphology and dye staining) when text-based disentanglement is impossible. The Dyed Lifted Polyp class encodes two co-occurring attributes (polyp morphology and indigo carmine staining) with no undyed counterpart in the training MedSteer: Counterfactual Endoscopic Synthesis 7 Table 2: Counterfactual generation (UC: Ulcerative Colitis, Esoph.: Esophagitis). Bg metrics are not applicable for pairs (B) and (C): the polyp segmentor cannot produce valid masks for diffuse mucosal inflammation or esophageal anatomy. Re-prompting is excluded because it rerolls the trajectory and produces different anatomy. (A) Polyp Norm. Cecum (B) UC Normal Cecum (C) Esoph. DDR = dye detection rate (lower = dye removed). Eff. LPIPS = LPIPS/(1DDR) (). Eff. SSIM = (1DDR)SSIM, Eff. PSNR = (1DDR)PSNR (). Segformer UNet++ Method Eff. LPIPS Eff. SSIM Eff. PSNR DDR Eff. LPIPS Eff. SSIM Eff. PSNR h-Edit PnP 0.4540 0.3021 0.0957 0.1872 2.78 4.93 0.900 0. 0.5190 0.3235 0.0952 0.1865 2.73 4.82 MedSteer 0.2027 0. 15.57 0.250 0.2193 0.6521 15.18 distribution, making text-based disentanglement impossible. We construct dye pathology vector from Tpos=dyed lifted polyps and Tneg=polyps. Because the pair differs only in dye presence, the resulting vector is orthogonal to polyp morphology by construction, suppressing only the dye attribute while preserving polyp structure. MedSteer achieves dye detection rate (DDR, fraction of outputs still classified as dyed) of 0.250, substantially outperforming h-Edit (DDR = 0.900) and PnP (DDR = 0.800) (Table 3 and Fig. 2). MedSteer also natively supports compositional edits (e.g., Dyed Lifted Polyp Normal Cecum) with strict anatomical consistency.

sections.title: 4.5 Ablation Studies
sections.content: Table 4 sweeps layer windows at fixed α=2.5, showing that layers 816 are the semantic formation zone. Windows entirely outside this range yield near-zero flip, and width-12 window at 012 achieves only 37.7% flip vs. 70.0% for 820, confirming position effect. Table 4 shows flip rises sharply from α=2.0 (51.3%) to α=2.5 (80.0%) and degrades at α=3.0 (70.0%). The pathology vector stabilises after 50 seeds  (Table 4)  . Per-token cosine similarity scores σl,t provide built-in spatial explanation, with the activation footprint being broad at early diffusion steps and contracting to sparse patches by the final steps. 8 Pham et al. Warmer colours indicate stronger alignment with the pathology vector. Right: corresponding unsteered (dyed lifted polyps) and steered (normal cecum) endoscopy images. 4.

sections.title: Interpretability
sections.content: The per-token cosine similarity score σl,t can be rendered directly as spatial map, yielding built-in visualisation of where the model steers at each diffusion step. As Fig. 3 shows, the activation footprint covers nearly the entire image at early steps and becomes progressively sparser toward the final steps, reflecting that fewer tokens require adjustment once the concept has been established. Inversion-based baselines have no analogous interpretable gate.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: We presented MedSteer, training-free steering framework for medical image synthesis. It extracts pathology vectors from contrastive prompt pairs in the crossattention space of frozen diffusion transformer, then steers activations via cosine similarity to produce counterfactuals, with no source images, DDIM inversion, mask annotations, or retraining. On Kvasir v3 and HyperKvasir, MedSteer outperforms baselines on downstream polyp detection, counterfactual generation, and dye disentanglement. Additionally, per-token cosine similarity provides builtin spatial interpretability at each diffusion step. Future work will extend to 3D volumetric data, video endoscopy sequences, and cross-institutional deployment. MedSteer: Counterfactual Endoscopic Synthesis
